out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A direct link showing how stopword distribution statistics affect tokenizer design, vocabulary selection, or compression ratios in large-scale LLM training would make this relevant.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: It turns out the most ignored words in language break every statistical law linguists thought they knew — but your podcast audience almost certainly doesn't need to know that.

out_of_scope.title: MUTEX: Leveraging Multilingual Transformers and Conditional Random Fields for Enhanced Urdu Toxic Span Detection
out_of_scope.abstract: Urdu toxic span detection remains limited because most existing systems rely on sentence-level classification and fail to identify the specific toxic spans within those text. It is further exacerbated by the multiple factors i.e. lack of token-level annotated resources, linguistic complexity of Urdu, frequent code-switching, informal expressions, and rich morphological variations. In this research, we propose MUTEX: a multilingual transformer combined with conditional random fields (CRF) for Urdu toxic span detection framework that uses manually annotated token-level toxic span dataset to improve performance and interpretability. MUTEX uses XLM RoBERTa with CRF layer to perform sequence labeling and is tested on multi-domain data extracted from social media, online news, and YouTube reviews using token-level F1 to evaluate fine-grained span detection. The results indicate that MUTEX achieves 60% token-level F1 score that is the first supervised baseline for Urdu toxic span detection. Further examination reveals that transformer-based models are more effective at implicitly capturing the contextual toxicity and are able to address the issues of code-switching and morphological variation than other models.
out_of_scope.why_now: Urdu toxic span detection is an underserved NLP problem, and a first supervised baseline has some value to low-resource NLP researchers.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The method is XLM-RoBERTa + CRF — a well-worn sequence labeling recipe applied to a new language; there is no architectural novelty and no connection to the podcast's core lenses.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow language-specific application with no transferable method contribution
Memory/storage score is a false positive — zero systems relevance
Standard fine-tuning + CRF pipeline, nothing new for broad AI practitioners
60% token-level F1 as a first baseline signals immaturity of the dataset, not a strong result
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A cross-lingual toxic span detection framework that generalizes across script families or introduces a novel annotation/training approach would warrant reconsideration.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Why is detecting hate speech word-by-word in Urdu so hard — and does throwing a multilingual transformer at it actually help?

out_of_scope.title: MOOSEnger -- a Domain-Specific AI Agent for the MOOSE Ecosystem
out_of_scope.abstract: MOOSEnger is a tool-enabled AI agent tailored to the Multiphysics Object-Oriented Simulation Environment (MOOSE). MOOSE cases are specified in HIT ".i" input files; the large object catalog and strict syntax make initial setup and debugging slow. MOOSEnger offers a conversational workflow that turns natural-language intent into runnable inputs by combining retrieval-augmented generation over curated docs/examples with deterministic, MOOSE-aware parsing, validation, and execution tools. A core-plus-domain architecture separates reusable agent infrastructure (configuration, registries, tool dispatch, retrieval services, persistence, and evaluation) from a MOOSE plugin that adds HIT-based parsing, syntax-preserving ingestion of input files, and domain-specific utilities for input repair and checking. An input precheck pipeline removes hidden formatting artifacts, fixes malformed HIT structure with a bounded grammar-constrained loop, and resolves invalid object types via similarity search over an application syntax registry. Inputs are then validated and optionally smoke-tested with the MOOSE runtime in the loop via an MCP-backed execution backend (with local fallback), translating solver diagnostics into iterative verify-and-correct updates. Built-in evaluation reports RAG metrics (faithfulness, relevancy, context precision/recall) and end-to-end success by actual execution. On a 125-prompt benchmark spanning diffusion, transient heat conduction, solid mechanics, porous flow, and incompressible Navier--Stokes, MOOSEnger achieves a 0.93 execution pass rate versus 0.08 for an LLM-only baseline.
out_of_scope.why_now: Domain-specific agents with RAG and execution-in-the-loop are an active area, and the 0.93 vs 0.08 pass rate delta shows real value in structured-input repair pipelines.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: MOOSE is a niche computational physics framework; the method is too tightly coupled to HIT syntax and MOOSE's object registry to be instructive for a general AI audience without significant abstraction.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow application paper — audience is MOOSE practitioners, not the AI community broadly
Memory/storage lens is absent — no connection to KV cache, bandwidth, offload, or scheduling
Benchmark is 125 prompts on a single framework; generalizability undemonstrated
Core architectural ideas (RAG + tool agents + grammar repair) are not novel; the novelty is domain-specific engineering
Episode would require extensive MOOSE context-setting before any payoff
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A follow-up showing the core-plus-domain architecture generalizing across multiple structured-input domains (e.g., OpenFOAM, Abaqus, HPC job scripts) with a systematic comparison would justify a general episode on domain-specific coding agents.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What happens when you point an LLM at a domain with strict, opaque syntax — and how far deterministic repair loops and execution feedback can take you.

out_of_scope.title: GALACTIC: Global and Local Agnostic Counterfactuals for Time-series Clustering
out_of_scope.abstract: Time-series clustering is a fundamental tool for pattern discovery, yet existing explainability methods, primarily based on feature attribution or metadata, fail to identify the transitions that move an instance across cluster boundaries. While Counterfactual Explanations (CEs) identify the minimal temporal perturbations required to alter the prediction of a model, they have been mostly confined to supervised settings. This paper introduces GALACTIC, the first unified framework to bridge local and global counterfactual explainability for unsupervised time-series clustering. At instance level (local), GALACTIC generates perturbations via a cluster-aware optimization objective that respects the target and underlying cluster assignments. At cluster level (global), to mitigate cognitive load and enhance interpretability, we formulate a representative CE selection problem. We propose a Minimum Description Length (MDL) objective to extract a non-redundant summary of global explanations that characterize the transitions between clusters. We prove that our MDL objective is supermodular, which allows the corresponding MDL reduction to be framed as a monotone submodular set function. This enables an efficient greedy selection algorithm with provable $(1-1/e)$ approximation guarantees. Extensive experimental evaluation on the UCR Archive demonstrates that GALACTIC produces significantly sparser local CEs and more concise global summaries than state-of-the-art baselines adapted for our problem, offering the first unified approach for interpreting clustered time-series through counterfactuals.
out_of_scope.why_now: Counterfactual explainability for unsupervised clustering is an open academic problem, and the MDL + submodular framing is theoretically clean.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: Neither editorial lens applies: no connection to memory/storage systems, and the audience is narrow XAI/time-series practitioners rather than the broad AI community.
downgrade_reasons: Memory lens absent — no KV cache, bandwidth, quantization, or scheduling relevance
Narrow domain: time-series clustering XAI does not transfer to modern LLM or systems work
UCR Archive benchmarks are academic, not production-scale AI workloads
No impact on how people build, train, serve, or evaluate modern AI systems
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Applying counterfactual cluster-boundary analysis to embedding spaces or attention patterns in large transformer models would create a genuine bridge to the podcast's audience.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could show an AI model the minimum edit to its input that would flip which cluster it belongs to — and GALACTIC does this for time-series without any labels?

out_of_scope.title: ConTSG-Bench: A Unified Benchmark for Conditional Time Series Generation
out_of_scope.abstract: Conditional time series generation plays a critical role in addressing data scarcity and enabling causal analysis in real-world applications. Despite its increasing importance, the field lacks a standardized and systematic benchmarking framework for evaluating generative models across diverse conditions. To address this gap, we introduce the Conditional Time Series Generation Benchmark (ConTSG-Bench). ConTSG-Bench comprises a large-scale, well-aligned dataset spanning diverse conditioning modalities and levels of semantic abstraction, first enabling systematic evaluation of representative generation methods across these dimensions with a comprehensive suite of metrics for generation fidelity and condition adherence. Both the quantitative benchmarking and in-depth analyses of conditional generation behaviors have revealed the traits and limitations of the current approaches, highlighting critical challenges and promising research directions, particularly with respect to precise structural controllability and downstream task utility under complex conditions.
out_of_scope.why_now: Conditional time series generation lacks standardized evaluation, and this benchmark could help practitioners compare methods in domains like finance and healthcare.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a narrow domain benchmark with no connection to either editorial lens — it neither addresses broad LLM/AI systems topics nor touches memory, KV cache, scheduling, or inference infrastructure.
downgrade_reasons: Memory/storage connection absent — not related to AI memory systems
Narrow application benchmark, not a general AI methods paper
No implications for how modern large models are built, trained, or served
Low transferability to the podcast's core audience interests
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the benchmark evaluated generative models on memory-constrained or streaming hardware settings, or if the conditioning methods had clear transfer to LLM sequence modeling.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: How do you know if your time series generator actually learned the right patterns — and why does the field still lack a standard answer?

out_of_scope.title: Embedded Inter-Subject Variability in Adversarial Learning for Inertial Sensor-Based Human Activity Recognition
out_of_scope.abstract: This paper addresses the problem of Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using data from wearable inertial sensors. An important challenge in HAR is the model's generalization capabilities to new unseen individuals due to inter-subject variability, i.e., the same activity is performed differently by different individuals. To address this problem, we propose a novel deep adversarial framework that integrates the concept of inter-subject variability in the adversarial task, thereby encouraging subject-invariant feature representations and enhancing the classification performance in the HAR problem. Our approach outperforms previous methods in three well-established HAR datasets using a leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) cross-validation. Further results indicate that our proposed adversarial task effectively reduces inter-subject variability among different users in the feature space, and it outperforms adversarial tasks from previous works when integrated into our framework. Code: https://github.com/FranciscoCalatrava/EmbeddedSubjectVariability.git
out_of_scope.why_now: Adversarial domain adaptation for wearable sensor HAR is a mature sub-field with no pressing reason for a broad AI audience to engage with it now.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The method is standard adversarial domain adaptation applied to a narrow sensor classification task; it does not generalize to modern large-model AI systems or any memory/storage concern.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow application domain (wearable HAR)
No connection to memory, storage, KV cache, or AI systems
Adversarial domain adaptation is well-established prior art
Results confined to small-scale sensor datasets
No relevance to LLMs, foundation models, or inference systems
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: Show that the inter-subject variability framing transfers to domain adaptation challenges in foundation model fine-tuning or personalization at scale.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: Can adversarial training teach a model to ignore who you are and just recognize what you're doing — and what does that tell us about generalization in AI?

out_of_scope.title: Towards a data-scale independent regulariser for robust sparse identification of non-linear dynamics
out_of_scope.abstract: Data normalisation, a common and often necessary preprocessing step in engineering and scientific applications, can severely distort the discovery of governing equations by magnitudebased sparse regression methods. This issue is particularly acute for the Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) framework, where the core assumption of sparsity is undermined by the interaction between data scaling and measurement noise. The resulting discovered models can be dense, uninterpretable, and physically incorrect. To address this critical vulnerability, we introduce the Sequential Thresholding of Coefficient of Variation (STCV), a novel, computationally efficient sparse regression algorithm that is inherently robust to data scaling. STCV replaces conventional magnitude-based thresholding with a dimensionless statistical metric, the Coefficient Presence (CP), which assesses the statistical validity and consistency of candidate terms in the model library. This shift from magnitude to statistical significance makes the discovery process invariant to arbitrary data scaling. Through comprehensive benchmarking on canonical dynamical systems and practical engineering problems, including a physical mass-spring-damper experiment, we demonstrate that STCV consistently and significantly outperforms standard Sequential Thresholding Least Squares (STLSQ) and Ensemble-SINDy (E-SINDy) on normalised, noisy datasets. The results show that STCV-based methods can successfully identify the correct, sparse physical laws even when other methods fail. By mitigating the distorting effects of normalisation, STCV makes sparse system identification a more reliable and automated tool for real-world applications, thereby enhancing model interpretability and trustworthiness.
out_of_scope.why_now: SINDy-based sparse regression is an active area in scientific ML, and robustness to data normalization is a real pain point for practitioners in physics-informed modeling.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This paper targets computational scientists doing equation discovery from dynamical systems, not AI practitioners building or running neural network systems; the method and benchmarks have no pathway into modern deep learning workflows.
downgrade_reasons: SINDy is not used in neural network training, serving, or evaluation
Memory/storage connection is absent — no KV cache, quantization, or data movement relevance
Benchmarks are canonical ODE systems and a mass-spring-damper, not AI workloads
Audience is scientific computing / physics-informed ML, not the podcast's listener base
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If STCV were applied to sparse attention or weight pruning in transformer training with competitive results on standard NLP/vision benchmarks.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if the reason your physics-informed ML model is wrong isn't your data — it's how you normalized it?

out_of_scope.title: AILS-NTUA at SemEval-2026 Task 10: Agentic LLMs for Psycholinguistic Marker Extraction and Conspiracy Endorsement Detection
out_of_scope.abstract: This paper presents a novel agentic LLM pipeline for SemEval-2026 Task 10 that jointly extracts psycholinguistic conspiracy markers and detects conspiracy endorsement. Unlike traditional classifiers that conflate semantic reasoning with structural localization, our decoupled design isolates these challenges. For marker extraction, we propose Dynamic Discriminative Chain-of-Thought (DD-CoT) with deterministic anchoring to resolve semantic ambiguity and character-level brittleness. For conspiracy detection, an "Anti-Echo Chamber" architecture, consisting of an adversarial Parallel Council adjudicated by a Calibrated Judge, overcomes the "Reporter Trap," where models falsely penalize objective reporting. Achieving 0.24 Macro F1 (+100\% over baseline) on S1 and 0.79 Macro F1 (+49\%) on S2, with the S1 system ranking 3rd on the development leaderboard, our approach establishes a versatile paradigm for interpretable, psycholinguistically-grounded NLP.
out_of_scope.why_now: SemEval shared task papers are timely during competition windows, and adversarial adjudication architectures for reducing model sycophancy have general interest.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: This is a competition system description for a highly specific NLP task; 0.24 Macro F1 on S1, despite doubling the baseline, is not a result that signals a broadly useful method.
downgrade_reasons: Narrow shared-task paper: conspiracy endorsement detection
No memory/storage relevance whatsoever
SemEval system papers rarely transfer beyond the competition
Methods (DD-CoT, adversarial council) are incremental, not novel enough to anchor an episode
Psycholinguistic marker extraction is not a topic the podcast audience follows
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: A paper applying adversarial multi-agent adjudication to reduce hallucination or improve calibration on general-purpose LLM benchmarks would be worth revisiting.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you made two AIs argue with each other to stop them from penalizing honest reporting — and used it to hunt conspiracy theories?

out_of_scope.title: A Geometry-Adaptive Deep Variational Framework for Phase Discovery in the Landau-Brazovskii Model
out_of_scope.abstract: The discovery of ordered structures in pattern-forming systems, such as the Landau-Brazovskii (LB) model, is often limited by the sensitivity of numerical solvers to the prescribed computational domain size. Incompatible domains induce artificial stress, frequently trapping the system in high-energy metastable configurations. To resolve this issue, we propose a Geometry-Adaptive Deep Variational Framework (GeoDVF) that jointly optimizes the infinite-dimensional order parameter, which is parameterized by a neural network, and the finite-dimensional geometric parameters of the computational domain. By explicitly treating the domain size as trainable variables within the variational formulation, GeoDVF naturally eliminates artificial stress during training. To escape the attraction basin of the disordered phase under small initializations, we introduce a warmup penalty mechanism, which effectively destabilizes the disordered phase, enabling the spontaneous nucleation of complex three-dimensional ordered phases from random initializations. Furthermore, we design a guided initialization protocol to resolve topologically intricate phases associated with narrow basins of attraction. Extensive numerical experiments show that GeoDVF provides a robust and geometry-consistent variational solver capable of identifying both stable and metastable states without prior knowledge.
out_of_scope.why_now: Neural networks as PDE solvers is a live research area, and joint optimization of network weights and domain geometry is a clean methodological contribution.
out_of_scope.why_not_higher: The paper solves a condensed matter physics problem (Landau-Brazovskii phase discovery); the cs.LG tag reflects the tool, not the domain, and there is no path to AI systems relevance.
downgrade_reasons: Domain is materials science / condensed matter physics, not AI
Memory score driven by keyword overlap, not actual memory-systems content
Method does not transfer to training, inference, or serving of AI models
No AI audience hook beyond 'we used a neural network'
out_of_scope.what_would_raise_priority: If the geometry-adaptive variational framework were applied to a core AI problem—e.g., adaptive tokenization, mesh-based attention, or continuous latent space optimization—it could re-enter as a methods paper.
out_of_scope.one_sentence_episode_hook: What if you could let a neural network redesign the geometry of its own problem domain mid-training—and would that ever matter outside of physics?
